content:
3 min read
Just now

--

I’ve always found the Rubik’s Cube fascinating — not just as a puzzle, but as a genuinely elegant piece of engineering. So a few weeks ago I decided to combine three things I wanted to get better at — computer vision, algorithms, and 3D graphics — into a single project: a web app that scans a real Rubik’s Cube through your laptop webcam, figures out the fastest way to solve it, and then walks you through every move using an interactive 3D cube on screen.
The Idea
The core concept is simple to describe, even if it wasn’t simple to build: hold your physical cube up to the camera, show each of the six faces, and let the app take it from there. No manually clicking in 54 colored squares, no typing in cube notation. Just scan, verify, and solve.
Once the app has “seen” the entire cube, it calculates the optimal sequence of moves using Kociemba’s Algorithm — the same two-phase algorithm speedcubers and solving programs rely on to get from a scrambled cube to a solved one in as few moves as possible. From there, a 3D cube rendered in the browser animates each move in sequence, so you can follow along on your real cube one twist at a time.
How It Actually Works
Seeing the cube. The webcam feed comes in through the MediaDevices API, and each frame gets processed on an HTML canvas. From there, the app samples the color of each of the nine stickers on a face and classifies them into the six standard cube colors. Lighting, glare, and slightly-off colors are the enemy here — getting consistent detection across different rooms, cameras, and cube brands took a good amount of trial and error.
Making sure the cube is real. Color detection alone isn’t enough — you also need to confirm what you’ve scanned is actually a valid, solvable cube state. Rubik’s Cubes have strict internal constraints (certain color combinations and piece orientations simply can’t exist), so the app validates the full cube state before handing it off to the solver. This step also gives users a chance to double check and correct any face the camera misread.
Solving it. Once validated, the cube state is fed into the solver (built on top of the cubejs library, which implements Kociemba’s two-phase algorithm) to generate an efficient move sequence — typically well under 25 moves, regardless of how scrambled the cube is.
Showing it. This is where Three.js comes in. Rather than just printing a list of moves like “R U R’ U’”, the app renders a fully interactive 3D cube that physically rotates the correct layer for each step, so you can watch the move happen rather than decode notation in your head.
What I Learned
This project ended up being a much deeper dive into JavaScript than I expected. A few highlights:

• Computer vision is mostly about handling the messy cases. Detecting color under ideal lighting is easy; detecting it reliably across different webcams, room lighting, and slightly worn cube stickers is where the real work happens.

• 3D animation forces you to think in state, not just visuals. Every rotation had to stay perfectly in sync with the actual logical cube state, or the animation would drift out of alignment with the real solve.

• Good UX matters even in “technical” projects. Letting users verify and fix misread colors turned out to be just as important as the detection algorithm itself — trust in the tool matters more than raw accuracy.
Tech Stack

• HTML / CSS / JavaScript — core structure and logic

• Three.js — 3D cube rendering and move animation

• cubejs — cube state representation and Kociemba’s algorithm implementation

• MediaDevices API — webcam access

• Canvas API — frame capture and color sampling
Wrapping Up
What started as “can I get a webcam to recognize cube colors?” turned into a full pipeline spanning computer vision, algorithmic problem solving, and real-time 3D rendering — all running entirely in the browser. It’s the kind of project that’s small enough to finish in your spare time, but touches enough different disciplines that you come out the other side having learned a lot more than you expected.
If you’re looking for a project idea that mixes a bit of everything, I can’t recommend this one enough.
https://github.com/kulatshreeram/rubiks-cube-solver.git
